events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is a retired industrial engineer, former smoker (30 pack-year history, quit 15 years ago), with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with lisinopril and metformin.

At age 63 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, cT3bN1M1) CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA (ICD-O-3 histology code 8310/3; primary site: right kidney, ICD-O-3 site code C64.0). TNM staging: T3b (tumor extends into renal vein), N1 (regional retroperitoneal lymph node involvement), M1 (bilateral pulmonary metastases). Summary stage: Distant. Tumor size: 11.4 cm greatest dimension. Sarcomatoid differentiation: present, estimated 20% of tumor volume. Relevant diagnostic information: IMDC risk score: Intermediate risk (2 factors: <1 year from diagnosis to systemic therapy, thrombocytosis). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY. Laterality: Right. Fuhrman/WHO-ISUP nuclear grade: Grade 4. Presenting symptoms: gross hematuria x 3 weeks, right flank pain, 15 lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months. Serum calcium: 10.9 mg/dL (slightly elevated). Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL (anemia). Platelet count: 478,000/uL (thrombocytosis). Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio: 4.2. LDH: 287 U/L (mildly elevated). KPS: 80.

At age 63 years, the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (staging CT), which showed a large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 11.4 x 9.8 x 10.1 cm with central necrosis, arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney; the mass extends into the right renal vein without definite IVC involvement. Multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes were identified including a 2.8 cm right para-aortic node and a 1.9 cm aortocaval node. Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease were present, the largest measuring 1.4 cm in the right lower lobe; additional nodules 0.8 cm left lower lobe and 0.6 cm right upper lobe. No hepatic, osseous, or adrenal metastases identified. Cancer present. Metastatic sites: bilateral pulmonary nodules, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

At age 63 years, the patient had an MRI ABDOMEN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST (pre-surgical planning), which confirmed an 11.4 cm right renal mass with involvement of the right renal vein (renal vein thrombus extending to but not into the IVC). No Gerota fascia invasion identified. The right adrenal gland appears separate from the mass. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy confirmed. No evidence of IVC thrombus. Cancer present. Metastatic sites: right renal vein thrombus, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

At age 63 years (pre-surgical biopsy, CT-guided), the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY on a core needle biopsy specimen from the right renal mass. Diagnosis: CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION (estimated 20% of sampled tissue). Immunohistochemistry: PAX8 positive, CA-IX diffusely positive, CD10 positive, vimentin positive, CK7 focally positive, TFE3 negative. Ki-67 proliferation index: 35%. PD-L1 (SP142): Combined positive score (CPS) 15, tumor proportion score (TPS) 10%. Tumor necrosis: present.

At age 63 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for clear cell renal cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right renal mass (core needle biopsy). Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) showed: ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS: VHL p.L89fs*4 (frameshift deletion, pathogenic, loss of function, VAF 48%); PBRM1 p.Q357* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, loss of function, VAF 42%); BAP1 p.R227* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, loss of function, VAF 39%) -- presence of both PBRM1 and BAP1 loss is notable, BAP1 loss associated with higher grade and sarcomatoid features. ADDITIONAL ALTERATIONS: SETD2 p.R1625* (loss of function, VAF 31%); KDM5C p.Y611* (loss of function, VAF 22%); CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion; MET amplification (copy number 6.8); TP53 p.R248W (gain of function, VAF 18%); TERT promoter C228T mutation (VAF 35%). Copy number alterations: chromosome 3p loss (encompassing VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, SETD2 loci), chromosome 9p loss (CDKN2A/B), chromosome 14q loss, chromosome 5q gain. Tumor mutational burden: 6.2 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS. No FGFR alterations. No KRAS, NRAS, or BRAF mutations detected. No ALK, ROS1, or RET fusions. PD-L1 expression (22C3): CPS 18. Relevant negative findings: no HIF2A mutation, no TSC1/TSC2 mutations, no NF2 mutation.

At age 63 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment. The case was reviewed by medical oncology, urology/surgical oncology, radiation oncology, and radiology. Consensus recommendation: given resectable primary tumor with limited metastatic burden (oligometastatic pulmonary disease and retroperitoneal nodal involvement), cytoreductive nephrectomy (CN) followed by systemic immunotherapy-based combination is recommended. Patient is IMDC intermediate risk. Rationale for CN: tumor burden reduction, symptom relief (hematuria, flank pain), and pathologic confirmation of sarcomatoid component. Planned systemic therapy post-CN: ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks x 4 doses, then nivolumab maintenance. Cancer present. No response assessment yet -- pre-treatment.

At age 63 years, the patient underwent CYTOREDUCTIVE RIGHT RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY WITH RETROPERITONEAL LYMPH NODE DISSECTION (open approach). Procedure: right radical nephrectomy with en bloc excision of Gerota fascia, adrenalectomy (right), and template retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. Renal vein thrombus excised. Estimated blood loss: 950 mL; 2 units pRBC transfused intraoperatively. Operative time: 4.5 hours. No intraoperative complications. Surgical margins: negative (R0 resection of primary). Lymph nodes: 8 nodes retrieved, 3 positive (3/8). Pathologic stage: ypT3bN1 (post-cytoreductive, metastatic disease remains).

At age 63 years (surgical specimen), the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY on the right radical nephrectomy specimen. Final diagnosis: CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, WHO/ISUP grade 4 (highest grade present), with SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION comprising 22% of viable tumor volume (revised from biopsy estimate). Tumor size: 11.6 x 10.2 x 9.7 cm. Lymphovascular invasion: present. Perineural invasion: absent. Renal vein involvement: positive (tumor thrombus). Renal sinus fat invasion: present. Perinephric fat invasion: present. Gerota fascia: not involved. Surgical margins: all negative. Right adrenal gland: not involved by tumor. Lymph nodes: 3 of 8 retroperitoneal nodes positive for metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid features. Tumor necrosis: extensive (>50%). Immunohistochemistry on sarcomatoid component: vimentin positive, focal desmin positive, CK AE1/AE3 focally positive, PD-L1 (22C3) CPS 22 in sarcomatoid areas.

At age 63 years (4 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient had a post-surgical oncology assessment. Recovery was unremarkable. Surgical wound healing well. Creatinine: 1.6 mg/dL (baseline pre-op 1.1; expected after nephrectomy). Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (recovering). Patient reports resolution of hematuria and improvement in flank pain. Performance status: KPS 80, ECOG 1. Decision to proceed with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg q3 weeks x 4 cycles followed by nivolumab 480 mg q4 weeks maintenance. Patient counseled extensively on immune-related adverse events. Cancer present (pulmonary and nodal metastases remain). Pre-treatment response assessment.

At age 63 years, the patient received IPILIMUMAB 1 mg/kg + NIVOLUMAB 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (combination immunotherapy, induction phase, 4 planned cycles) for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation, IMDC intermediate risk, first-line setting. Treatment intent: palliative/disease control with potential for durable response. Premedications: diphenhydramine, acetaminophen. Cycle 1 administered without complications.

At age 63 years (after cycle 2 of ipilimumab/nivolumab, approximately 6 weeks into immunotherapy), the patient developed IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENT: HYPOTHYROIDISM (grade 2, CTCAE v5.0). TSH: 18.4 mIU/L (elevated), free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (low). Patient reported fatigue and cold intolerance. Management: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily initiated; immunotherapy continued without dose modification per NCCN guidelines for grade 2 endocrinopathy. TSH trended toward normalization at 6-week follow-up. No steroids required.

At age 63 years (after completing 4 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction, approximately 3 months post-initiation), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (first restaging scan). Results showed MIXED RESPONSE (RECIST 1.1 modified): Target lesions -- right lower lobe pulmonary nodule decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm (36% decrease, partial response); left lower lobe nodule stable at 0.8 cm; right upper lobe nodule decreased from 0.6 cm to 0.4 cm. Non-target lesions (retroperitoneal nodal metastases) -- previously identified right para-aortic node region (post-lymphadenectomy) showed new 1.6 cm nodal mass in the right common iliac region not previously seen; aortocaval region: 2.1 cm new nodal conglomerate. Overall assessment: mixed response -- pulmonary lesions responding, but NEW retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease. No new hepatic, osseous, or CNS metastases. Cancer present, mixed response to immunotherapy.

At age 63 years (3 months into immunotherapy), the patient had an oncology assessment following first restaging imaging. The mixed response pattern was discussed with the patient: improving pulmonary disease but new retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal progression. Given the pattern (possible pseudoprogression vs. true discordant response), the MDT recommendation was to continue nivolumab maintenance given responding pulmonary lesions and to repeat imaging in 6 weeks. Patient's performance status: KPS 80, ECOG 1. Patient reports mild fatigue (ongoing, grade 1) and occasional nausea (grade 1). Tolerating immunotherapy well aside from hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine). Decision: continue nivolumab 480 mg q4 weeks. Cancer present; mixed response.

At age 63 years (after 4 induction cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient transitioned to NIVOLUMAB 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (maintenance monotherapy, flat dosing) for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma following completion of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction. Continuation of PD-1 blockade given mixed response with pulmonary improvement.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 months into immunotherapy, 6 weeks after first restaging scan), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (interim restaging). Results: Pulmonary target lesions continue to respond -- right lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (additional decrease), right upper lobe nodule 0.3 cm. Left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (slightly decreased). Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease: previously noted right common iliac nodal mass now 2.0 cm (increased from 1.6 cm); aortocaval conglomerate 2.4 cm (increased from 2.1 cm). New findings: 1.1 cm right obturator node. Overall: discordant response persisting -- pulmonary disease responding, nodal disease progressing. Not meeting RECIST criteria for overall progression due to pulmonary response offsetting nodal growth. Cancer present; discordant/mixed response.

At age 63 years (6 months into immunotherapy), the patient had a multidisciplinary review of ongoing discordant response. Pulmonary disease responding well to nivolumab; however nodal disease showing consistent progression. The sarcomatoid component of the tumor (22%) was felt to potentially represent an immunotherapy-resistant subclone driving the nodal progression, while the conventional clear cell component responds in the lungs. Decision: continue nivolumab for additional 6 weeks with repeat imaging; if nodal disease continues to progress, transition to cabozantinib. Patient counseled regarding potential need for treatment change. Performance status: KPS 75, ECOG 1. Cancer present; mixed response with nodal progression.

At age 63 years (approximately 9 months into immunotherapy), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (restaging, approximately 9 months post-initiation of systemic therapy). Results: Pulmonary lesions: right lower lobe 0.8 cm (stable/minimal increase from 0.7 cm); left lower lobe 0.8 cm (stable); right upper lobe 0.3 cm (stable). Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease: right common iliac nodal mass 3.1 cm (increased from 2.0 cm); aortocaval conglomerate 3.4 cm (increased from 2.4 cm); right obturator node 1.8 cm (increased from 1.1 cm). New finding: right external iliac node 1.2 cm. No new pulmonary nodules. No hepatic metastases. No bone metastases. Overall RECIST assessment: PROGRESSIVE DISEASE (nodal disease sum of diameters increased >20% from nadir; overall tumor burden growing). Cancer present; disease progression on nivolumab (nodal progression).

At age 63 years (9 months after initiating first-line systemic therapy), the patient had an oncology assessment confirming RECIST progression on nivolumab maintenance. Patient has been on systemic therapy for 9 months total (ipilimumab/nivolumab x 4 cycles then nivolumab maintenance). Best response was mixed (PR in lungs, PD in nodes). Now with clear nodal progression. Patient reports new bilateral lower extremity edema (grade 1), pelvic discomfort, and worsening fatigue (grade 2). Performance status: KPS 70, ECOG 2. Labs: creatinine 1.8 mg/dL; ALT 32, AST 28 (normal); no thyroid symptoms on levothyroxine. Decision: discontinue nivolumab; initiate second-line CABOZANTINIB 60 mg orally once daily (standard starting dose for RCC). Rationale: cabozantinib targets VEGFR, MET, and AXL -- MET amplification identified on NGS, which may predict enhanced sensitivity. Patient educated on TKI side effects, particularly hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, hypertension, and fatigue. Cancer present; progressive disease.

At age 63 years (9 months after initial diagnosis, second-line), the patient received CABOZANTINIB 60 mg orally once daily for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, second-line after progression on ipilimumab/nivolumab. MET amplification on NGS noted as potential biomarker of sensitivity. Treatment intent: palliative/disease control. Dose: 60 mg/day (standard). Anti-hypertensive therapy adjusted (lisinopril dose increased to 20 mg daily given anticipated TKI-related hypertension).

At age 63 years (approximately 3 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient developed HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, HFS; CTCAE grade 2). Symptoms: painful erythema, swelling, and blistering on bilateral palmar surfaces and plantar aspects of feet bilaterally, limiting instrumental activities of daily living. Management: cabozantinib dose reduced to 40 mg orally once daily; urea 20% cream twice daily to affected areas; gel cushion insoles recommended; patient instructed to avoid tight footwear and prolonged walking. Patient also reported DIARRHEA (CTCAE grade 2, 4-6 stools above baseline per day) beginning concurrently; managed with loperamide 4 mg initial dose then 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day), low-residue diet counseling, and oral hydration reinforcement.

At age 63 years (5 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient was evaluated for HYPERTENSION (CTCAE grade 2) with blood pressure readings 158/96 mmHg and 162/98 mmHg on two separate occasions. Patient's antihypertensive regimen augmented: amlodipine 5 mg daily added to existing lisinopril 20 mg. Blood pressure at 2-week follow-up: 142/88 mmHg (improved but not yet at goal). Amlodipine uptitrated to 10 mg daily. Cabozantinib continued at 40 mg without further dose modification. HFS improving on dose reduction and topical therapy (grade 1). Diarrhea improving (grade 1, manageable with PRN loperamide).

At age 63 years (6 weeks into cabozantinib), the patient had an oncology office visit. Patient tolerating cabozantinib 40 mg daily with manageable toxicities. HFS now grade 1 (mild erythema, no blistering). Diarrhea grade 1 (2-3 loose stools per day above baseline, managed with dietary modification and PRN loperamide). Hypertension improving on dual antihypertensive therapy. Fatigue grade 1. No hepatotoxicity (LFTs normal). TSH within normal range on levothyroxine. Performance status: KPS 75, ECOG 1 (improved from ECOG 2 at TKI initiation). Patient denies mucositis or dysgeusia. No new lower extremity edema. Cancer present (by most recent imaging); clinical response assessment pending first cabozantinib restaging scan.

At age 63 years (approximately 3 months after initiating cabozantinib, first restaging on TKI), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Results: Pulmonary lesions: right lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (stable/slight decrease from 0.8 cm); left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (stable); right upper lobe nodule stable at 0.3 cm. Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease: right common iliac nodal mass decreased from 3.1 cm to 2.3 cm; aortocaval conglomerate decreased from 3.4 cm to 2.6 cm; right obturator node decreased from 1.8 cm to 1.4 cm; right external iliac node 0.9 cm (previously 1.2 cm). Overall RECIST assessment: PARTIAL RESPONSE in nodal disease (sum of target lesion diameters decreased 27% from baseline at cabozantinib initiation). No new lesions. No hepatic, osseous, or CNS metastases. Cancer present; responding to cabozantinib.

At age 63 years (3 months into cabozantinib), the patient had an oncology assessment following first restaging scan on TKI. Excellent response to cabozantinib with partial response in the progressive nodal disease. Pulmonary disease remains stable. Patient very encouraged by response. Toxicities continue to be manageable: HFS grade 1 (well-controlled with topical therapy and dose reduction); diarrhea grade 1 (controlled with PRN loperamide and dietary changes); hypertension controlled on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg (BP 138/82 today). Decision: continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily (no dose re-escalation given adequate disease control and manageable toxicities at current dose). Next restaging in 3 months. Continue levothyroxine. Cancer present; stable disease/partial response on cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (approximately 6 months after initiating cabozantinib, second restaging on TKI), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Results: Pulmonary lesions: right lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (stable); left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (stable); right upper lobe nodule 0.3 cm (stable). Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease: right common iliac nodal mass 2.2 cm (stable compared to 2.3 cm); aortocaval conglomerate 2.5 cm (stable); right obturator node 1.3 cm (stable); right external iliac node 0.8 cm (stable). No new lymphadenopathy. No new hepatic lesions. New finding: small 0.5 cm sclerotic focus in right iliac bone, indeterminate (too small to characterize, recommend bone scan). RECIST overall: STABLE DISEASE. Cancer present; stable disease on cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (bone scan following indeterminate iliac bone finding on CT), the patient had a WHOLE-BODY TECHNETIUM-99M BONE SCAN. Results: No convincing evidence of osteoblastic or osteolytic metastatic disease. The right iliac sclerotic focus corresponds to an area of mildly increased radiotracer uptake, likely reactive/degenerative etiology rather than metastatic disease. No other areas of abnormal uptake identified. Conclusion: No definitive bone metastases. Cancer status: no new metastatic sites confirmed.

At age 64 years (6 months into cabozantinib), the patient had an oncology assessment following second restaging scans on TKI therapy. Stable disease confirmed on cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Bone scan reassuring -- no osseous metastases. Patient's overall disease status: stable pulmonary and nodal disease. Performance status: KPS 80, ECOG 1. Current toxicities: HFS grade 1 (chronic, manageable with ongoing topical therapy); diarrhea grade 1 (2 loose stools per day above baseline, well-managed); hypertension controlled (BP 136/80 today on current regimen). No significant fatigue, mucositis, or hepatotoxicity. Patient is working part-time and maintaining quality of life. Labs: creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (stable); normal LFTs; TSH 2.8 mIU/L (therapeutic). Plan: continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily; maintain current supportive medications; restage in 3 months; continue monitoring for emerging resistance or new metastatic sites. Cancer present; stable disease -- best response to second-line cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (approximately 9 months after initiating cabozantinib; 18 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a routine oncology office visit. Patient reports he has been tolerating cabozantinib 40 mg daily continuously. Reports chronic grade 1 HFS requiring daily topical urea cream and careful foot care; intermittent grade 1 diarrhea managed with dietary adjustments. Voice slight dysgeusia (grade 1 taste changes) that has been present for several months, affecting appetite but not causing significant weight loss (weight stable at 178 lbs, down from 198 lbs at diagnosis). Blood pressure well-controlled. No new symptoms. ECOG performance status: 1. Labs: CBC, CMP, LFTs within normal limits (creatinine 1.8 mg/dL stable). Plan: proceed with CT restaging as scheduled. Continue current cabozantinib dose and supportive measures. Quality of life acceptable; patient engaged with survivorship counseling. Cancer present; ongoing stable disease assessment pending.

scenario_blurb: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma stage IV with sarcomatoid differentiation (20%) in a 63-year-old, presenting with large right renal mass, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and pulmonary metastases. Undergoes cytoreductive nephrectomy. First-line ipilimumab/nivolumab with mixed response. Progression at 9 months, switched to cabozantinib with stable disease. Manages TKI-related hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea.
